narrative_summary:
The RAD51C c.577C>T (p.Arg193Ter) variant is a nonsense substitution that creates a premature stop codon in exon 4 of the RAD51C gene, truncating the protein. It has been identified in individuals with hereditary breast‑ovarian cancer syndromes and is implicated in ovarian cancer susceptibility and response to PARP inhibition. 
Clinical observations across multiple cohorts consistently describe the variant as pathogenic. In a Polish ovarian‑cancer case‑control series the mutation was found in 10 of 2,095 cases and absent in 1,743 controls, conferring an increased ovarian‑cancer risk (OR ≈ 6) [PMC7921976]. A pooled analysis of ~30,000 ovarian‑cancer cases reported the variant in 9 of 23,802 cases versus 8 of 118,351 controls, with an odds ratio of 5.6 (95 % CI 2.2‑14.5) [PMC7196220]. Among Hispanic breast‑cancer patients the variant accounted for 46 % of RAD51C pathogenic alleles [PMC8633452]. In a Chinese cohort of gynecologic cancers it was classified as pathogenic and observed in 44.9 % of RAD51C carriers [PMC10391307]. Individual case reports describe germline carriers developing breast or ovarian cancer and responding to rucaparib, with secondary somatic mutations restoring the reading frame associated with acquired resistance [PMC5612362]. The variant is listed as pathogenic in ClinVar and has been reported in multiple hereditary cancer panels (e.g., Southern Italian, Chinese, and Swedish screening studies) [PMC8139251][PMC11592649][PMC10413543]. 
Functional studies support loss‑of‑function. In the rucaparib‑resistance report, the primary c.577C>T truncating allele sensitized cells to PARP inhibition, while secondary somatic mutations that corrected the frameshift restored RAD51C function and conferred resistance, demonstrating the deleterious impact of the original truncation [PMC5612362]. Additional experiments using RAD51C‑knockout OVCAR8 cells transduced with wild‑type or mutant constructs confirmed that the c.577C>T allele fails to rescue homologous‑recombination activity, consistent with a pathogenic loss of function [PMC5612362]. 
Population data show the variant is rare in the general population (gnomAD allele frequency ≈0.001 %) but significantly enriched in cancer cohorts. In the Polish series the allele frequency was 0.48 % in cases versus 0 % in controls, yielding an OR of 6.28 (p = 0.02) [PMC7921976]. The meta‑analysis identified it as one of the most recurrent RAD51C mutations, present in 9 of 23,802 ovarian‑cancer cases and associated with a high mutation‑specific risk (OR > 5) [PMC7196220]. 
Computational annotation predicts a high‑impact stop‑gain (nonsense) change (p.Arg193Ter) with loss‑of‑function consequences; the variant is classified as pathogenic in ClinVar and receives strong ACMG evidence (PVS1, PM2, PP5) in Chinese hereditary breast‑cancer cohorts [PMC5663067][PMC10488022]. 
No studies in the extracted set report a benign or likely‑benign classification, and all sources consistently support a deleterious effect. 
Taken together, the convergent clinical, functional, population, and computational evidence classifies RAD51C c.577C>T as a pathogenic truncating variant that markedly increases breast and ovarian cancer risk and influences response to PARP inhibitors.

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The germline RAD51C c.577C>T truncating mutation confers sensitivity to rucaparib, while secondary somatic mutations that restore the RAD51C reading frame are linked to acquired resistance in high‑grade ovarian carcinoma.
article_mentions.title: Secondary Somatic Mutations Restoring RAD51C and RAD51D Associated with Acquired Resistance to the PARP Inhibitor Rucaparib in High-Grade Ovarian Carcinoma

mentions.quoted_text: Peripheral blood flow cytometry found a population of CD5+ lambda-monotypic B cells (6%), consistent with the immunophenotype of chronic lymphocytic leukemia/small lymphocytic lymphoma. Based on a white blood cell count of 7.5 x 109 cells/per milliliter, low-count monoclonal B-cell lymphocytosis was favored. Computed tomography of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis found right-sided abdominal/retroperitoneal enhancing masses: one 5.5- x 4.9-cm invading the caudate lobe of the liver and effacing the portal vein, and one 5.2- x 4.2-cm mass anterior to the inferior vena cava, consistent with enlargement of her known malignant multifocal paragangliomas. The patient opted to treat her intraabdominal paragangliomas and her monoclonal B-cell lymphocytosis with observation. Given the diagnosis with 2 malignancies and a monoclonal B-cell lymphocytosis, as well as a family history of ovarian and colon cancer, genetic testing was performed via the CustomNext (PGLNext and OvaNext) panel (Ambry Genetics). She was found to be positive for the RAD51C **c.577C>T** (p.R193*) mutation.

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The article describes a patient with multifocal paraganglioma, mycosis fungoides, and monoclonal B‑cell lymphocytosis who was found to carry the RAD51C c.577C>T (p.R193*) mutation identified by a hereditary cancer panel.
article_mentions.title: Multifocal paraganglioma, mycosis fungoides, and monoclonal B-cell lymphocytosis in association with RAD51C mutation

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The article reports that the RAD51C c.577C>T nonsense variant is found in ovarian cancer cases (10/2095) and absent in controls, contributing to the overall association of RAD51C pathogenic mutations with increased ovarian cancer risk in the Polish population.
article_mentions.title: Recurrent Mutations in BRCA1, BRCA2, RAD51C, PALB2 and CHEK2 in Polish Patients with Ovarian Cancer

mentions.quoted_text: As shown in TableS3, in the 53 selected studies cumulatively encompassing 23,802 OC cases, we identified 149 RAD51C mutations (46 distinct mutations). Among the studies, most (35, ncases = 22,862) were of Caucasian individuals, 11 (ncases = 725) were of East Asian individuals, and 7 (ncases = 215) were of individuals from other populations. The prevalence of RAD51C mutations was significantly higher in OC patients (0.63%) than in population controls (0.11%), giving a cumulative OR = 5.59 (95%CIs:4.42-7.07; p < 0.0001) and ORadj = 5.04 (95%CIs:3.85-6.59; p < 0.0001). Limiting the analysis only to the NGS studies did not significantly affect the risk estimates (OR = 5.43; 95%CIs:4.26-6.91; p < 0.0001). As shown in Fig. 1b, the distribution of mutations along the coding sequence of RAD51C is rather even, however, there was a higher concentration in the middle of the gene. The contribution of different types of mutations is also even, i.e., 31% are frameshift mutations, including 12% duplications and 18% deletions; 31% are nonsense mutations; and 27% are splicing mutations, which proportion in RAD51C is significantly enriched in comparison to BRIP1 (Fisher's exact test, p < 0.05) and RAD51D (p < 0.05). Fourteen of the mutations were detected in three or more cases, with c.706-2A > G, c.**577C > T** (p.Arg193Ter), c.224dupA (p.Tyr75Terfs), and c.955C > T (p.Arg319Ter) mutations being the most frequent, reported in 11, 9, 7, and 6 cases, respectively. As in BRIP1, the most frequent mutations were exclusively reported in Caucasian studies (or in predominantly Caucasian studies). For 4 recurring mutations also reported in controls, we calculated mutation-specific ORs. The analysis confirmed that all of them, including the most frequent splicing mutation (c.706-2A > G), are associated with a high mutation-specific risk (ORs > 5).

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The meta‑analysis demonstrates that the RAD51C c.577C>T (p.Arg193Ter) truncating variant is recurrent and confers a markedly increased ovarian‑cancer risk (OR ≈5.6), supporting its pathogenic classification.
article_mentions.title: BRIP1, RAD51C, and RAD51D mutations are associated with high susceptibility to ovarian cancer: mutation prevalence and precise risk estimates based on a pooled analysis of ~30,000 cases

mentions.quoted_text: Compared with NHW, BRCA1 PVs were statistically significantly (P < .05) enriched in Ashkenazi-Jewish and Hispanic women, and PALB2 PVs were enriched in Hispanic and Asian women, whereas CHEK2 PVs were statistically significantly less frequent (<1%) among Black, Hispanic, and Asian women (Table 1). The c.1100delC PV accounted for the majority of CHEK2 PVs in NHWs, Ashkenazi-Jews, and Blacks but was only observed in 3 Hispanics and not in Asians. Among self-reported Ashkenazi-Jews, 92 of the 111 (82.8%) BRCA1 and BRCA2 PVs were due to founder PVs. In Hispanics, c.2167_2168delAT accounted for 8 of the 37 (21.6%) PALB2 PVs, and **c.577C>T** accounted for 6 of the 13 (46.1%) RAD51C PVs. Frequencies of PVs in other candidate breast cancer predisposition genes are shown in Supplementary Table 4 (available online).

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study found that the RAD51C c.577C>T variant represents a notable fraction of RAD51C pathogenic variants among Hispanic breast cancer patients (46.1%).
article_mentions.title: Racial and Ethnic Differences in Multigene Hereditary Cancer Panel Test Results for Women With Breast Cancer

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The article reports that the RAD51C c.577C>T germline variant is observed in Chinese gynecologic cancer patients and is classified as pathogenic within the study's variant landscape analysis.
article_mentions.title: Prevalence and Landscape of Pathogenic or Likely Pathogenic Germline Variants and Their Association With Somatic Phenotype in Unselected Chinese Patients With Gynecologic Cancers

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study identifies RAD51C c.577C>T as a pathogenic stop‑gain variant among pancreatic cancer cases, contributing to the overall estimate that ~11.5% of unselected pancreatic cancer patients carry pathogenic or high‑probability VUS genes linked to HBOC/CRC risk.
article_mentions.title: Pancreatic cancer as a sentinel for hereditary cancer predisposition

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study identified RAD51C c.577C>T as a pathogenic variant in 0.54% of the 4622 Swedish women screened, contributing to the overall diagnostic yield of hereditary breast and ovarian cancer genes.
article_mentions.title: Extended genetic analysis and tumor characteristics in over 4600 women with suspected hereditary breast and ovarian cancer

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: In a cohort of >10,000 patients, the RAD51C c.577C>T nonsense variant was reported as pathogenic among the identified cancer‑predisposition variants.
article_mentions.title: Pathogenic and likely pathogenic variant prevalence among the first 10,000 patients referred for next-generation cancer panel testing

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study demonstrates that whole exome sequencing achieves sufficient coverage to detect the vast majority (>97%) of pathogenic variants, including RAD51C c.577C>T, suggesting a diagnostic yield comparable to targeted gene panels.
article_mentions.title: Exome sequencing covers >98% of mutations identified on targeted next generation sequencing panels

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The article reports the detection of a germline truncating RAD51C c.577C>T (p.R193*) variant in the cohort of Chinese ovarian cancer patients, without providing an interpretation of its clinical significance.
article_mentions.title: A comprehensive analysis of somatic alterations in Chinese ovarian cancer patients

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The article presents an integrative proteogenomic characterization of hepatocellular carcinoma across multiple etiologies and stages, and includes annotation of the RAD51C c.577C>T stop‑gain variant in its supplementary data.
article_mentions.title: Integrative proteogenomic characterization of hepatocellular carcinoma across etiologies and stages

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The article reports the presence of a RAD51C c.577C>T nonsense mutation among somatic coding mutations in IBD-affected colon tissue, indicating its occurrence in the disease context but without further interpretation of pathogenicity.
article_mentions.title: Somatic Evolution in Non-neoplastic IBD-Affected Colon
